sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Ergonomic assessment aims to reduce avoidable load, awkward posture, and repetitive strain while keeping the worker functional. It should be specific to the real task, not a generic checklist alone.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Neck, shoulder, elbow, wrist, or back symptoms linked to desk, vehicle, plant, or control-room work
- Symptoms worsen with prolonged static posture or repetitive input
- Reports of awkward monitor, seat, keyboard, reach, or visibility demands
- Reduced tolerance for shift duration or driving

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Ergonomics should complement, not replace, active rehabilitation and load tolerance building
- A comfortable setup does not eliminate the need to move regularly
- In remote sites, vehicle fit, vibration, seat time, and comms or control equipment may matter as much as the office desk

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Identify the main aggravating tasks and whether immediate changes can reduce exposure
- Encourage movement breaks, postural variation, and temporary task modification
- Escalate for major neurological symptoms or if symptoms suggest non-ergonomic pathology

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Review seat, monitor, controls, hand tools, reach zones, visual demands, and break structure in the actual work environment if possible
- Recommend practical changes that the workplace can implement, not idealised setups that cannot be maintained
- Pair ergonomic advice with strengthening, pacing, and exposure planning

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The best workstation is one that fits the job, the worker, and the real constraints of the site
